California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
Michael J. Kersten
Deputy Legislative counsel
Office of Legislative Counsel
3 021 State capitol.
Sacramento, CA 95814
Dear Mr. Kersten:
February 2■′ ■990
Re:  Your Request For lnfo..lla■ Assistance
Our File No. 
■-90-054
ity
lega1 advisor rather
treating your
to
You have requested advice with respect [民
ct'テ. aS:illl・i:g
provisions of the Po■
itica■ Refo■Ill Act (th
are requesting advice in your capacity as lega1
than on behalf of a specific individual' we are
than on behalf of a specific
reguest as one for informal assistance pursuant
ne{ulation L823s (c) .2
OUESTION
Are the three sample letters provided "substantially sinilartt
for purposes of the uass nailing restrictions of the Act?
CONCLUSTON
The three letters are trsubstantially siroilar'r for purposes of
the mass mailing restrictions. 
No nore than a total of 200 copies
of the letters may be sent at public expense in a single calendar
month.
1 co.r".rrr"nt code Sections 81ooo-91015. A11 statutory references
are to the Government code unless otherrrise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18ooo, et seo. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the california code of Regrulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor wj.th the
inrnunity provided by an opinion or fornal written advice.
(covernment code section 83114; 2 cal . code of Regs.
section 18329 (c) (3) . )
428 J Street,Suite 800 0 P.0.Box 807 0 Sacramento CA 95804Ю807 ● (916)322‐5660


Our File No. I-90-054
Page 2
FACTS
Your office is often requested to review proposed nailings on
behalf of MeEbers of the Legislature. You have enclosed three
sample letters and wish to know if these letters would be
considered rrsubstantially sinilarrr for purposes of the nass mail-
ing restrictions. 
(Section 89001; Regulation 18901, copy
enclosed.) Copies of the sanple letters are attached. For
convenient reference, they are labeled #L, #2, and #3.
ANALYSTS
Section 8900I prohibits the sending of nass uailings at
public expense. The conmission has interpreted Section 89001 as
applying only to nailings containing the narnes or other references
to elected officers. 
The letters that you have provided reference
the elected officers who will be sending then by use of pronouns
such as rrlrr and "uyrr and rrould be signed by the elected officer.
If the letters will be sent at public expense to the recipient,s
resj,dence, place of enployment or business, or post office box,
they fal1 within the prohibition on nass uailing unless one of the
available exceptions applies. 
(Regrulation 18901(a).)
Section 82041.5 defines a mass uailing, in part, as rrover two
hundred substantiallv sinilar pieces of raiI....tr 
(Emphasis
added.) Regulation 18901, adopted by the Cornmission on
December 13, 1989,5 defines "substantial,Iy sinilarrr as follows:
(A) lwo itens are trsubstantially sirj.larrr if
any of the following applies:
(i) 
The itens are identical, except for
changes necessary to identify the recipient
and his or her address.
(ii) 
The iteDs are intended to honor,
conmend, congratulate, or recognize an
individual or group, or individuals or groups,
for the sane event or occasion; are intended
to celebrate or recognize the sane holiday; or
are intended to congratulate an individual or
group, or individuals or groups, on the sane
t)rpe of event, such as birthdays or an-
niversaries .
3 thi. regulation is in the rulenaki.ng process. I{e advise that
the regulation be followed while it is undergoing review by the
Office of Adninistrative Law, and we anticipate that it will be in
effect in the near future.
〕


Our Fi■e No. ■-90-054
Page 3
(iii) 
Both of the follorring apply to the
i.tens rnai led:
a. uost of the bilIs, legislation,
governmental aetion, act j,vities, events,
or issues of public concern uentioned in
one iten are mentioned in the other.
b. l,lost of the inf onnation
contained in one iten is contained in the
other.
(B) Enclosure of the sane j.nfornational
materials in two itens nailed, such as copies of
the same bi1l, public document, or report, shall
not, by itself, 
nean that the two items are
rrsubstantially sirnilar.'r 
such infornational
materials may not include the elected officer's
name, photograph, signature, or any other reference
to the elected officer except as pernitted
elselrhere in this regulation.
Regulation 18901(c) (3) .
Since the letters provided are neither identical nor
congratulatory, they uill only be considered substantially sinilar
if they neet the 2-part test set forth in subdivision
(c) (a) (l) ( iii) 
.
1. Do the letters nention nost of the sane bilIs. leqisla-
tion, qovernmental action. activities, 
events, or issues?
A11 three letters Dention the saBe governmental action to
equalize school funding in the state budget so as to give more
money to schools.
Letter #1: 'rDuring the recent budget hear j-ngs, uy colJ.eagues
and I passed a nei, funding systen which will begin to equalize o1d
disparities. 
This net, system will give [blank] schools an ad-
ditional [bIank] nillion in funds next year.rl
Letter #2: rr...I led an effort to equalize the funding for
categorical programs. This effort was successful, gaining ap-
proval by the Legislature as part of the budget hearings.
students in [blank] can now expect an additi.onal [blank] nillion
in funding for the 1989-90 school year.r'
Letter #3: rr...I developed a proposal to begin to equatize
per pupil spending. My proposal will use soDe of the new uoney
approved by the voters rrith Proposition 98 to bring [blank]
schools up to the statewide average in categorical funding. My
proposal has been adopted....This will give [b1ank] about [blank]
more for our schools. rr


Our Fi■e No. I-90-054
Page 4
2. Is most of the same infornation contained in both let-
ters?
ALI three letters contain the following basic inforaration:
I. 
Categorical prograns/ funding provide money for special
programs .
2. The money is not divided equally.
3. Los Angeles schools get about $1,ooo/student compared to
lblankl provided to [blank].
4. The new system will give lblank] Doney to [blank].
The letters differ in some respects.
t. 
Letters #1 and #2 say that the categorical funding goes
to children with special needs such as the gifted or those with
English lang'uage problems. Letter #3 says the funds are used for
pet projects of special interests.
2. Letter #I says the rnoney goes nostly to large districts.
Letter #l says the rnoney goes to Large, urban districts at the
expense of suburban districts. 
Letter 12 nakes no similar direct
stateDent.
3. Letter #2 provides additional specific facts such as the
size of the education budget (over S24 billion), 
the number of
categorical proqrams in the state (75), and the state average
spending for categorical support ($454).
Notuithstanding the above differences, we would
conservatively advise that most of the infornation in the three
letters is the sane, and that the letters are therefore
substantially siniJ.ar.
of course, this does not nean that the letters nay not be
sent. ft merely neans that nultlple copies of the letters may not
be sent so that the total sent in a calendar month exceeds 200.
Alternatively, the infornation nay be sent to unlirnited
persons if all references to the elected officer are removed or if
no public funds are used to pay for the cost of distribution and
no Dore than $50 of public funds are used to pay for desigm,
product j,on, or printing. 
(Regulation 1980I(a) (3).)


Our Fl■e No. I-90-054
Page 5
I trust that this letter will be of assistance to you in
promulgating advice to llembers of the Leqislature. If you have
any additional questions in this regard, you may contact me at
(916) 322-590]-.
s incerely,
Kathryn E. Donovan
General counsel
By: l,largaret w. Ellison
counsel, Legal Division
